findings.description: XSS in SampleAjaxPlugin - user-controlled data (actionName and params) reflected directly to HTTP response without HTML encoding
findings.code_snippet: response.getWriter().print( "You called! actionName=" + actionName + " params=" + params );
findings.reasoning: The SampleAjaxPlugin.service() method receives actionName and params directly from the HTTP request (via WikiAjaxDispatcherServlet) and reflects them verbatim into the HTTP response without any HTML encoding. The response is written via PrintWriter which defaults to text/html content type, making this a reflected XSS. An attacker can craft a request to the Ajax endpoint with a malicious actionName or param value containing JavaScript.

evidence.code_context: response.getWriter().print( "You called! actionName=" + actionName + " params=" + params );
evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiAjaxDispatcherServlet (line 135) gets request params via AjaxUtil, passes them as actionName/params to SampleAjaxPlugin.service() at line 59 which writes them directly to response without encoding.
evidence.notes: The actionName and params come from HttpServletRequest query parameters. No HTML encoding is applied before writing to response.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted request to the SampleAjaxPlugin Ajax endpoint with XSS payload in actionName, which is reflected directly in the response body
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The SampleAjaxPlugin must be registered/accessible via the WikiAjaxDispatcherServlet endpoint

findings.description: XSS in CookieTag - cookie value written directly to JSP output without HTML encoding
findings.reasoning: CookieTag reads a cookie value (either directly via cookie.getValue() at line 245, or via getItemValue() which parses cookie contents) and writes it directly to the JSP page output without HTML encoding. An attacker who can set a cookie value containing JavaScript (e.g., through a subdomain cookie injection or another vulnerability) can trigger XSS when that cookie value is rendered on the page. The cookie value flows from line 245 (cookie.getValue()) to line 260 (pageContext.getOut().print(out)) without any encoding. Additionally, there is a second path where cookie values are read from the session (line 320-341) and also output without encoding.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Cookie value obtained at line 245 via getCookie().getValue() flows directly to pageContext.getOut().print() at line 260 without HTML entity encoding. No TextUtil.replaceEntities() or similar encoding is applied.
evidence.notes: The second code flow also shows cookie data flowing through getItemValue() (lines 320-341) which parses cookie values and also reaches the same output sink at line 260.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker who can set a cookie (e.g., via network position, MITM, or subdomain cookie injection) stores an XSS payload in a cookie value that CookieTag renders without encoding
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to set a cookie with an XSS payload in its value; the page must use the CookieTag without the m_var attribute (writing directly to output)

findings.description: XSS in CalendarTag - HTTP query string embedded directly in HTML output without encoding
findings.code_snippet: out.write( ""+ getMonthNaviLink(prevCal,"<<", queryString)+ ""+ getMonthLink( cal )+ ""+ getMonthNaviLink(nextCal,">>", queryString)+ "\n" );
findings.reasoning: The CalendarTag retrieves the HTTP query string from the request at line 280 via HttpUtil.safeGetQueryString(), which returns the raw query string (only stripping the 'page=' parameter but not HTML-encoding it). This queryString is then passed to getMonthNaviLink() and embedded directly into HTML output at lines 281-287. An attacker can craft a URL with a malicious query string containing HTML/JavaScript that will be reflected in the calendar navigation links without HTML encoding. This is confirmed by Query 3 (xss-html-concat) which specifically flags this pattern of user data embedded in HTML string concatenation.

evidence.code_context:
Line 280: final String queryString = HttpUtil.safeGetQueryString(httpServletRequest, engine.getContentEncoding());
Lines 281-287: out.write('<tr>'+getMonthNaviLink(prevCal,'<<', queryString)+...getMonthNaviLink(nextCal,'>>', queryString)+'</tr>');
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HttpUtil.safeGetQueryString() at line 199 gets request.getQueryString() at line 218, returns raw query string. CalendarTag line 280 gets this value, line 282/286 passes it to getMonthNaviLink() which embeds it in HTML links, then lines 281-287 write to out without HTML encoding.
evidence.notes: The HttpUtil.safeGetQueryString only re-encodes bytes for character encoding but does NOT HTML-encode the query string characters. The resulting HTML is written directly to the output stream.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL with XSS payload in query parameters; when a victim visits the page containing the CalendarTag, the query string is reflected in the calendar navigation HTML without encoding
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The wiki page must contain a CalendarTag and the victim must visit the attacker-crafted URL

findings.description: XSS in FeedDiscoveryTag - site name and RSS feed URLs embedded in HTML link tags without full encoding
findings.code_snippet: pageContext.getOut().print("<link rel=\"alternate\" type=\"application/rss+xml\" title=\"RSS wiki feed for page "+siteName+".\" href=\""+rssFeedURL+"\" />\n");
findings.reasoning: FeedDiscoveryTag retrieves the siteName from Feed.getSiteName(m_wikiContext) which derives from wiki variables (ultimately from request parameters via DefaultVariableManager). While siteName is passed through TextUtil.replaceEntities() at line 50, the rssFeedURL at line 47 is constructed with encodedName which is page.getName() encoded via engine.encodeName() - this is URL encoding, not HTML entity encoding. The rssFeedURL is embedded directly inside an HTML attribute value (href) without HTML encoding. An attacker who can control the page name with HTML special characters (like ") could break out of the attribute context. Similarly, rssURL at line 52 is embedded in href without HTML encoding.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiContext.getHttpRequest() at line 473 provides request, DefaultVariableManager at line 200/202 reads variables. Feed.getSiteName() at line 63/71 returns wiki variable value. FeedDiscoveryTag at line 49/50 gets siteName, applies replaceEntities (HTML encoding for siteName is OK), but rssFeedURL at line 53 and rssURL at line 52 are embedded in href attributes without HTML encoding - engine.encodeName() is URL encoding not HTML encoding.
evidence.notes: siteName is properly HTML-encoded at line 50, but the URL values (rssURL, rssFeedURL) are embedded in href attributes without HTML attribute encoding. Also lines 65-66 and 68-69 have similar issues with blogFeedURL and atomFeedURL.

exploit_payload.scenario: If the wiki page name or RSS URL contains HTML special characters, they get injected into HTML link tag attributes without encoding, enabling XSS
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to create a wiki page with a name containing HTML special characters, or control the global RSS URL

findings.description: XSS in FeedDiscoveryTag - blog feed URL and atom feed URL embedded in HTML href attributes without HTML encoding
findings.reasoning: Similar to line 53, the blogFeedURL (line 63) and atomFeedURL (line 64) are constructed from the page name via engine.getURL() and encodeName(), which performs URL encoding but not HTML entity encoding. These URLs are then embedded in HTML href attribute values without HTML encoding. If the page name contains HTML special characters like double-quotes, they could break out of the attribute context. These URLs flow from the wiki context/request parameters through engine.getURL() into the HTML output.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: encodedName comes from page.getName() via engine.encodeName() (URL encoding only). blogFeedURL/atomFeedURL constructed with these, then embedded in HTML href attributes at lines 65-69 without HTML entity encoding.
evidence.notes: Same vulnerability pattern as line 53, but for weblog-specific links.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a weblog-enabled wiki page with a specially crafted name containing HTML characters; the blog/atom feed discovery links in the page header contain unencoded XSS payload
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to create weblog wiki pages with names containing HTML special characters

findings.description: AuthorTag - author name written to JSP output - FALSE POSITIVE: author goes through wiki rendering pipeline which HTML-encodes output
findings.reasoning: The author variable at line 68 comes from one of two paths: (1) When the author is a wiki page (lines 61-66), it goes through mgr.getHTML() which renders the wiki markup and returns HTML. The content "[author|author]" is wiki markup that renders a link - this goes through the full wiki rendering pipeline. (2) When the author is not a page (else branch), the raw author name is printed. For the wiki-rendered path, the output is already HTML. For the plain text path, the author name comes from a Principal.getName() which is typically a fixed string, not user-controlled in a meaningful XSS sense during page viewing. However, the author name IS stored as page metadata, and if a malicious author name was set, it could potentially be rendered. The main issue is that when the author IS a wiki page, the HTML is from the rendering pipeline. When not a wiki page, the plain name is printed. This is a borderline case - the author name from page metadata could contain XSS if not sanitized when stored.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: For wiki-page authors: author goes through RenderingManager.getHTML() which processes wiki markup through CreoleRenderer. The rendered HTML is then output. For non-wiki-page authors: raw Principal.getName() string is output - this is typically the username string.
evidence.notes: The wiki rendering pipeline (CreoleRenderer) may or may not sanitize all XSS. The dataflow in the query traces through WeblogPlugin -> TextUtil -> rendering pipeline, suggesting taint comes from wiki variables/request params.

findings.description: VariableTag - FALSE POSITIVE: variable value is properly HTML-encoded via TextUtil.replaceEntities() before output
findings.reasoning: The VariableTag at line 95 calls TextUtil.replaceEntities(value) before writing to output. This encodes HTML special characters (&, <, >, ", ') into HTML entities, which is the proper mitigation for HTML body context XSS. Even though the value comes from user-controlled wiki variables via DefaultVariableManager, the encoding prevents XSS. This is a false positive - the code properly sanitizes output.

evidence.code_context:
Line 81: value = engine.getManager(VariableManager.class).getValue(m_wikiContext, getVar());
Line 95: out.write(TextUtil.replaceEntities(value));
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Value comes from VariableManager, but TextUtil.replaceEntities() encodes HTML entities before writing to output. This is proper HTML context encoding.
evidence.notes: CodeQL may have flagged this because the taint flows to out.write(), but the encoding sanitizer TextUtil.replaceEntities() is applied inline.

findings.description: Stored XSS in BreadcrumbsTag - wiki page names from session breadcrumb list written to HTML anchor tags; page name is HTML-encoded but href URL is not
findings.reasoning: BreadcrumbsTag reads page names stored in the session breadcrumb queue (FixedQueue) and constructs HTML anchor links. The page name (curPage) used as link text is properly HTML-encoded via TextUtil.replaceEntities(). However, curPage is also passed to m_wikiContext.getViewURL(curPage) which constructs the href URL. If getViewURL() does not HTML-encode the page name before embedding it in the URL string, and the page name contains characters like ", it could break out of the href attribute. The breadcrumb list stores page names from previously visited pages, making this potentially a stored XSS if a malicious page name was created. The query 7 specifically flags this as stored XSS risk.

evidence.code_context: Line 160: curPage = trail.get(i); Line 165: out.print('' + TextUtil.replaceEntities(curPage) + '');
evidence.dataflow_analysis: curPage comes from session breadcrumb queue (FixedQueue). The link text is properly HTML-encoded with TextUtil.replaceEntities(), but the href is constructed from m_wikiContext.getViewURL(curPage) which may or may not HTML-encode the URL. If the URL contains unencoded characters like double-quotes, it breaks out of the attribute.
evidence.notes: The page name text is encoded, but the URL in href is the risk. This is a stored XSS since breadcrumbs come from session state.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a wiki page with a specially crafted name containing HTML attribute injection characters; when any user visits that page and navigates, the malicious page name is stored in their session breadcrumbs and rendered as an XSS payload
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to create wiki pages with names containing HTML special characters; victim must visit the attacker's page

findings.description: BugReportHandler - FALSE POSITIVE: plugin output goes through wiki markup rendering not directly to HTTP response
findings.code_snippet:
// Plugin execute() returns a String that is wiki markup, not direct HTTP response output
// The string is then rendered through the wiki rendering pipeline
findings.reasoning: BugReportHandler.execute() returns a String value that represents either wiki markup or plain text. The plugin framework (DefaultPluginManager at line 279, PluginContent at line 169) processes this return value. Looking at the code flow: the execute() method builds a wiki text string (lines 115-119 contain String construction with user params), but this output flows through the plugin execution framework which returns it as wiki content to be rendered. The sink is DefaultPluginManager.execute() returning to PluginContent which integrates the result into the wiki document. Plugin return values are treated as wiki markup or pre-rendered HTML depending on plugin type, but in this case the BugReportHandler redirects to a new page - the user-controlled data in the StringWriter (lines 115-119) is written to a new wiki page, not directly to the HTTP response. This is a false positive for XSS - the data goes to page storage, not HTTP response.

evidence.code_context: BugReportHandler builds text in StringWriter, writes it as content to a new wiki page via newPage/setContent. The plugin return value is a redirect or confirmation message, not raw user input.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Lines 115-119 show string building of wiki page content. The execute() method returns a success/error message string. The user input (title, description, etc.) is written to a new wiki page, not directly to HTTP response.
evidence.notes: Multiple code flows traced through DefaultPluginManager (line 279), PluginContent (lines 165-169) suggesting the plugin output is part of the wiki rendering system, not direct HTTP response writes.

findings.description: PluginTag - wiki plugin output written to JSP output - needs manual review to confirm if rendering pipeline sanitizes XSS
findings.reasoning: PluginTag executes a wiki plugin and writes the result directly to the JSP output. The result comes from DefaultPluginManager.execute() which runs the plugin's execute() method. The data source is wiki variables/request parameters going through WeblogPlugin and TextUtil. The key question is whether the wiki rendering pipeline (DefaultPluginManager at line 279) sanitizes plugin output before returning it. If plugins can return arbitrary HTML (which many do intentionally - they generate HTML markup), then user-controlled input flowing into a plugin's output could be XSS. The rendering pipeline processes the result but may not escape it since plugins are expected to return valid HTML. This needs manual review to confirm exploitation path.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Plugin output flows from request-controlled wiki variables through WeblogPlugin processing, then through DefaultPluginManager, and is written directly to JSP output. The rendering pipeline processes content but plugins return HTML strings.
evidence.notes: Plugins intentionally generate HTML output. Whether XSS is possible depends on whether the specific plugin being executed sanitizes user input before including it in returned HTML.

findings.description: InsertPageTag - rendered wiki page HTML written to JSP output - needs manual review to confirm if rendering pipeline sanitizes user wiki content
findings.code_snippet:
case HTML: out.print( engine.getManager( RenderingManager.class ).getHTML( m_wikiContext, insertedPage ) ); break;
case PLAIN: out.print( engine.getManager( PageManager.class ).getText( insertedPage ) ); break;
findings.reasoning: InsertPageTag writes either the rendered HTML of a wiki page or its plain text directly to JSP output. The PLAIN mode prints raw wiki markup without any HTML encoding, which could expose XSS if the wiki page content contains HTML tags. The HTML mode goes through the rendering pipeline (getHTML), which processes wiki markup but may allow certain HTML through if not sanitized. The data source traced is from wiki variables/request params through WeblogPlugin to DefaultPluginManager to RenderingManager. This needs manual review to determine if the wiki rendering pipeline prevents XSS in page content.

evidence.code_context:
Line 117: case HTML: out.print(engine.getManager(RenderingManager.class).getHTML(m_wikiContext, insertedPage));
case PLAIN: out.print(engine.getManager(PageManager.class).getText(insertedPage));
evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiContext.getHttpRequest() -> WeblogPlugin -> TextUtil -> DefaultPluginManager -> PluginContent -> RenderingManager -> InsertPageTag output
evidence.notes: PLAIN mode is particularly concerning as it would output raw wiki markup without encoding.

findings.description: WikiFormAdminBean - rendered wiki form HTML returned to admin panel without sanitization review
findings.reasoning: WikiFormAdminBean.doGet() retrieves a wiki form via getForm(context), parses it as wiki markup, renders it via mgr.getHTML(), and returns the result. The data source traced by CodeQL flows from WikiContext HTTP request through WeblogPlugin and TextUtil to the rendering pipeline. The rendered HTML is returned from doGet() to be displayed in the admin panel. If wiki form content can be influenced by user-controlled request parameters (via WeblogPlugin/DefaultVariableManager), this could result in XSS in the admin panel. This is a manual review case as the rendering pipeline behavior needs verification.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: WikiContext -> WeblogPlugin -> TextUtil -> DefaultPluginManager -> PluginContent -> CreoleRenderer -> DefaultRenderingManager -> WikiFormAdminBean.doGet() -> returned HTML
evidence.notes: Admin panel context means exploitation requires admin access, reducing severity.

findings.description: TranslateTag - FALSE POSITIVE: wiki markup is rendered through the wiki rendering pipeline which produces safe HTML output
findings.reasoning: TranslateTag reads wiki text from its JSP body content (bc.getString()) and passes it through the wiki rendering engine (RenderingManager.textToHTML()) to convert it to HTML. The result is then written to output. The wiki text comes from the JSP body tag content, not directly from request parameters. The rendering pipeline (textToHTML) processes wiki markup syntax and generates HTML - it is not expected to pass through raw HTML unless the wiki configuration explicitly allows HTML markup. The data flow traced by CodeQL goes through WeblogPlugin and DefaultPluginManager suggesting the taint comes from wiki variables in the page content rather than direct request parameters. This is a false positive since the wiki rendering pipeline is the intended mechanism for converting wiki markup to HTML, and the content originates from page body content rather than direct user request parameters.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: wikiText comes from JSP body content (bc.getString()), not directly from HTTP request. Goes through textToHTML() rendering pipeline before output.
evidence.notes: The rendering pipeline converts wiki markup syntax to HTML. The concern would be if the wiki engine allows raw HTML passthrough, but this is controlled by configuration, not the tag itself.

findings.description: PageNameTag writes page name via beautifyTitle() without HTML encoding, enabling XSS if page name contains HTML special characters
findings.reasoning: The PageNameTag writes the page name to the HTML output using `beautifyTitle()`, which calls `TextUtil.beautifyString()`. The `beautifyString` method only inserts spaces between words (e.g., for CamelCase formatting) — it does NOT HTML-encode special characters like `<`, `>`, `"`, or `&`. In contrast, the Attachment branch at line 45 does use `TextUtil.replaceEntities()` for HTML encoding. If a wiki page has a name containing HTML-unsafe characters (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`), this tag would write that name unencoded to the HTML output, triggering XSS in every page that uses this JSP tag. This is a stored XSS vulnerability since it reads from stored page names.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: beautifyTitle() → beautifyString() which only inserts spaces, no HTML entity encoding. If page name = '<script>alert(1)</script>', it is written directly to pageContext.getOut() (HTML response) without encoding.
evidence.notes: The Attachment case is correctly HTML-encoded, but the non-Attachment page name path is not. This inconsistency is a clear oversight.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a wiki page with a name containing HTML/JavaScript payload; the PageNameTag renders it unencoded in page titles causing stored XSS
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must have permission to create wiki pages with arbitrary names

findings.description: ParentPageNameTag writes parent page name via beautifyTitle() without HTML encoding at line 47 (attachment branch)
findings.reasoning: ParentPageNameTag at line 47 writes the parent page name of an Attachment via `beautifyTitle()` directly to the response writer. The `beautifyTitle()` method does not perform HTML entity encoding — it only calls `TextUtil.beautifyString()` which adds spaces between camelCase words. If the parent page name contains `<`, `>`, or `"` characters, those characters will be written verbatim into the HTML response, enabling stored XSS. Unlike `PageNameTag` where the attachment filename is properly encoded, here the PARENT name of an attachment is not encoded.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: beautifyTitle() → beautifyString() only inserts spaces. Attachment parent name or page name flows directly to HTML output without HTML entity encoding.
evidence.notes: Both branches of ParentPageNameTag are missing HTML encoding.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a wiki page with XSS payload in the page name and attaches a file; when the attachment page renders the <wiki:ParentPageName> tag, XSS fires
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must have permission to create wiki pages and upload attachments

findings.description: ParentPageNameTag writes non-attachment page name via beautifyTitle() without HTML encoding at line 60
findings.reasoning: The non-Attachment branch in ParentPageNameTag also uses `beautifyTitle()` without HTML encoding. The page name is processed to strip blog entry suffixes (`_blogentry_`) and comment suffixes (`_comments_`), but the remaining string is written unencoded via `beautifyTitle()`. If any part of the base page name contains HTML-unsafe characters, they will appear raw in the HTML response. This is a stored XSS vulnerability since the page name comes from the wiki page store.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: page.getName() → string manipulation → beautifyTitle() → beautifyString() → pageContext.getOut().print() without HTML encoding
evidence.notes: Both branches use beautifyTitle() which doesn't encode HTML entities.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a wiki page with an XSS payload name; the ParentPageNameTag renders it unencoded causing stored XSS
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must have permission to create wiki pages with arbitrary names

findings.description: BreadcrumbsTag writes getViewURL() result unencoded into href HTML attribute, enabling XSS if page name contains double-quote characters
findings.reasoning: The BreadcrumbsTag constructs an `href` attribute by concatenating `m_wikiContext.getViewURL(curPage)` directly into the HTML output without HTML entity encoding the URL. The URL is constructed from the page name via `WikiEngine.getURL()` → `urlConstructor.makeURL()`. If a page name contains `"` (double-quote), `>`, or JavaScript-injectable characters, the `href` attribute value could be broken out of. A URL containing `"` in a page name would allow attribute injection (e.g., `href="..."><script>...`). The inner text of the anchor (`curPage`) IS properly HTML-encoded with `TextUtil.replaceEntities()`, but the URL in the href is not. This enables a stored XSS attack if wiki page names can contain `"` or `>` characters that survive URL construction without being HTML-encoded.

evidence.code_context: BreadcrumbsTag.java lines 164-166: out.print( "" + TextUtil.replaceEntities( curPage ) + "" ); getViewURL calls getURL which calls urlConstructor.makeURL() - no HTML encoding on the URL before embedding in href attribute.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: curPage (page name from session) → getViewURL() → makeURL() → written raw into href=\"...\" without TextUtil.replaceEntities(). If page name contains '"', attacker can break out of href attribute.
evidence.notes: The link text IS encoded but the URL is not. This is an HTML attribute context injection risk. The URL needs HTML encoding (replacing '"' with ") when embedded in an attribute value.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a wiki page with a name containing quote characters, which gets added to other users' breadcrumb trails, triggering stored XSS when the breadcrumb is rendered
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to create wiki pages with names containing HTML-special characters, and the wiki must allow such characters in page names

findings.description: BreadcrumbsTag m_separator output is properly HTML-encoded via TextUtil.replaceEntities() in setSeparator()
findings.reasoning: The `m_separator` variable is set in `setSeparator()` via `m_separator = TextUtil.replaceEntities(separator)`. The HTML encoding is applied at assignment time, so by the time `m_separator` is written to output at line 169, its content is already HTML-encoded. This is not an XSS vulnerability.

findings.description: MessagesTag properly HTML-encodes all messages via TextUtil.replaceEntities() before writing to output
findings.reasoning: The MessagesTag builds its HTML output in a StringBuilder and every message is wrapped with `TextUtil.replaceEntities(message)` before being appended. Both the single-message path (line 91) and multi-message loop (line 97) apply HTML encoding. The final `sb.toString()` written at line 101 contains properly encoded content. This is not an XSS vulnerability.

findings.description: PageNameTag attachment filename is properly HTML-encoded via TextUtil.replaceEntities()
findings.reasoning: The attachment filename is passed through `TextUtil.replaceEntities()` which encodes `&`, `<`, `>`, and `"` into their HTML entity equivalents before writing to the response. This is properly sanitized and not vulnerable to XSS.

findings.description: UserNameTag properly validates username pattern and HTML-encodes via TextUtil.replaceEntities() before writing to output (line 57)
findings.reasoning: UserNameTag validates the user name against the regex pattern `^[^( |:)][^:]*` before printing. If the pattern matches, it passes the name through `TextUtil.replaceEntities()` for HTML encoding. If the pattern does not match, it outputs a static internationalized string instead. Both paths are safe. This is not a vulnerability.

findings.description: UserNameTag output at line 59-60 is either a properly HTML-encoded user name or a static i18n string - not vulnerable
findings.reasoning: This code path is reached when the username fails the pattern validation. It outputs a static i18n string from a resource bundle (not user-controlled), so there is no XSS risk here at all.

findings.description: UserProfileTag properly HTML-encodes user profile data via TextUtil.replaceEntities() before writing to output
findings.reasoning: The UserProfileTag applies `TextUtil.replaceEntities()` to the `result` string before writing it to the page output. This method encodes `&`, `<`, `>`, and `"` into their HTML entity equivalents, preventing XSS. All user profile properties (email, fullname, loginname, wikiname, groups, roles, dates) are routed through this encoding at line 154. This is not a vulnerability.
